summary: I was born in Seward, Nebraska, where my parents William and Laura Adams were farmers owning their own farm. It was a small-town upbringing that shaped my early life before my family moved to Bentondorf, Iowa. Seward is where my roots are, and it’s where my older brother Jerry stayed as a farmer while I eventually pursued pharmacy and military service.

My older brother Jerry served in the National Guard in Nebraska, which was part of our family's connection to military service. While I was drafted into the U.S. Army Medical Corps, Jerry's National Guard service was more localized and different from my active duty experience. It was interesting to see the different paths within military service in my family.

I served in the United States Army Medical Corps after being drafted during the Vietnam War. My role was primarily in the pharmacy at the 8th Field Hospital in Nha Trang, South Vietnam, where I helped fill prescriptions for soldiers and Vietnamese staff. It was a challenging but important part of the medical support system during the war, and I was proud to serve in that capacity.

I was drafted during the Vietnam War in 1968 and served about a year overseas in South Vietnam. My time there was mostly at the 8th Field Hospital in Nha Trang, where we dealt with medevac patients and occasional mortar attacks from the Viet Cong and North Vietnamese. The war was a tense and dangerous time, but I was fortunate not to be directly in combat, though the environment was still very challenging.

I began my military training at Fort Bliss, Texas, in July 1968 after being drafted out of Bentondorf, Iowa. The eight weeks of basic training there were rigorous, especially under the tough drill sergeant Sergeant Ray Kerr. Although I told my wife I never wanted to return to Texas, I ended up living there for 40 years, so it truly became home.

At the time I was drafted, I was living in Bentondorf, Iowa, having moved there from Nebraska. I was drafted out of the Iowa Draft Center, which sent me to Fort Bliss for training. Bentondorf was my home base before entering the Army and beginning my military journey.

Sergeant Ray Kerr was my drill instructor during basic training at Fort Bliss. He was a tough and demanding man whose job was to prepare us for combat, so he wasn’t gentle but treated us fairly. I remember him enforcing strict rules, like making me shave again during inspection because my beard was too long, which was a challenge for me as an older recruit.

After basic training, I was transferred to Fort Benning, Georgia, where I spent about nine to ten months. Fort Benning was primarily a tank and infantry training base, but since I was married, my wife and I were able to live off base in a small apartment, which made the living conditions more comfortable. It was a good transition period before my deployment to Vietnam.

I was stationed in Nha Trang, South Vietnam, at the 8th Field Hospital, which was about halfway down the country. Nha Trang was where I worked in the pharmacy, supporting both American soldiers and Vietnamese staff. The hospital was a medevac hub with helicopters frequently bringing in wounded soldiers, and it was also a place where I found moments of camaraderie and even participated in a choir.

The 8th Field Hospital in Nha Trang was where I served in Vietnam, working in the pharmacy. It was a busy place with constant medevac helicopters arriving with wounded soldiers. Despite the dangers, including mortar attacks from the Viet Cong and North Vietnamese, the hospital was a center of healing and community, where I even joined a choir organized by Chaplain David Fuller.

During my time at the 8th Field Hospital in Vietnam, we frequently came under mortar attacks from the Viet Cong. These attacks made the environment very dangerous, forcing us to take cover behind sandbags and remain vigilant. Although I was not on the front lines, the threat from the Viet Cong was a constant and serious concern for everyone at the hospital.

Alongside attacks from the Viet Cong, we also faced mortar attacks from forces associated with North Vietnam during my deployment. These attacks contributed to the tense and hazardous conditions at the 8th Field Hospital in Nha Trang. While I was not directly involved in combat, the presence of North Vietnamese forces was a constant threat during my service.

Mike McCrae was an enlisted pharmacy technician who worked alongside me at the 8th Field Hospital in Vietnam. We formed a strong friendship during our service and have kept in touch ever since. He lives in Ohio now and has even visited me and my family, which has been wonderful to reconnect after all these years.

David Fuller was the chaplain at the 8th Field Hospital where I served in Vietnam. He organized a choir that I volunteered for, which was a memorable and morale-boosting activity during our deployment. Recently, his son contacted me to share that David had received a Bronze Star Medal for bravery and meritorious service, which was a proud moment to hear about.

The Bronze Star Medal was awarded to Chaplain David Fuller for bravery and meritorious service during his time at the 8th Field Hospital in Vietnam. I learned about this honor when his son reached out to me years later to share the news and some pictures. It was a meaningful recognition of the chaplain’s dedication and courage during our deployment.

During my Vietnam service, I was fortunate to have a two-week rest period where I could fly to meet my wife, and we met in Hawaii. It was a welcome break from the intensity of the war zone and a chance to reconnect with family. Hawaii was a peaceful contrast to the challenges we faced in Vietnam.

After my military service, I was granted a slight early discharge so I could return to school to pursue an MBA. I went back to Lincoln, Nebraska, to attend graduate school and continue my education. This was an important step in my post-military career, and I balanced my studies with memories and keepsakes from my service, like the model ship I built in Vietnam.
